enrichment.short_description: MLOps platform for experiment tracking, model registry, and LLM observability, including the open-source Opik evaluation toolkit.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Comet offers ML experiment tracking, model registry, and LLM observability via Opik, with free, Pro, and Enterprise plans for teams of any size.

features.title: Experiment Tracking
features.description: Log, compare, and visualize machine learning experiments with custom visualizations.

features.title: Model Registry & Versioning
features.description: Version and manage models with a central registry as they move from experimentation to production.

features.title: Opik LLM Observability
features.description: Trace and debug complex GenAI systems and LLM applications with Comet's open-source Opik product.

features.title: LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation
features.description: 30+ built-in evaluation metrics for automated LLM output quality checks.

features.title: Test Suites
features.description: Pass/fail testing workflows for validating AI agent behavior before release.

features.title: Ollie Coding Agent
features.description: A built-in coding agent that analyzes traces and writes fixes directly to codebases.

features.title: Dataset Management
features.description: ML Artifacts for managing and versioning datasets across experiments.

features.title: Production Monitoring
features.description: Monitor deployed models and LLM applications for performance in production.

pros: Generous free tier for both the MLOps platform and Opik LLM observability, no credit card required
Open-source Opik version available on GitHub for self-hosting
Broad framework integrations spanning classic ML (PyTorch, TensorFlow, Scikit-learn) and LLM tooling (LangChain, LlamaIndex, OpenAI)
Enterprise plan offers SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, and GDPR compliance with flexible deployment
Used by large organizations including Uber, Netflix, and Autodesk, per Comet's website
cons: Two separate pricing structures (Opik vs. the core MLOps platform) can make cost planning less straightforward
Free plan usage caps (e.g., 25k spans/month for Opik, 100GB storage for MLOps) may limit larger teams
Enterprise pricing is custom/quote-only and not published
Some capabilities are gated behind Pro or Enterprise tiers

faqs.question: Does Comet offer a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both the Opik LLM observability product and the core MLOps platform have free tiers that don't require a credit card, though usage is capped, for example at 25k spans/month for Opik's Free Cloud plan.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Comet?
faqs.answer: Opik is available as open source on GitHub for self-hosting, and Comet also offers enterprise custom deployment options.

faqs.question: What is Opik?
faqs.answer: Opik is Comet's open-source AI observability and evaluation product for tracing, testing, and debugging LLM applications and AI agents.

faqs.question: What frameworks does Comet integrate with?
faqs.answer: Comet integrates with frameworks including PyTorch, TensorFlow, Scikit-learn, Hugging Face, LangChain, LlamaIndex, and OpenAI.

faqs.question: How much does Comet's paid plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro tier starts at $19/month for Opik Cloud or $19/user/month for the MLOps platform; Enterprise pricing is custom.

use_cases.title: LLM application debugging
use_cases.description: Teams building GenAI agents use Opik to trace, evaluate, and fix issues in production LLM calls.

use_cases.title: ML experiment tracking
use_cases.description: Data science teams log and compare model training runs, hyperparameters, and metrics.

use_cases.title: Model governance and registry
use_cases.description: Organizations version and track models as they move from experimentation to production.

use_cases.title: AI agent testing
use_cases.description: Teams use Test Suites and LLM-as-a-judge metrics to validate agent behavior before release.

features: Full AI observability & agent testing feature set
Customizable span limits and retention periods
SSO, SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR compliance
Experiment tracking, dataset management, model registry

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Comet?
paragraphs: Comet is an end-to-end MLOps and AI evaluation platform that helps teams track machine learning experiments, manage model versions, and observe LLM-based applications. Founded in 2017 and headquartered in New York, NY, Comet combines a traditional experiment tracking and model registry product with Opik, its open-source LLM observability and evaluation tool.

Opik provides tracing, LLM-as-a-judge evaluation metrics, test suites, and a built-in coding agent (Ollie) that analyzes traces and proposes code fixes. The MLOps side of the platform focuses on experiment tracking, dataset management via ML Artifacts, and model production monitoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Comet is for
paragraphs: Comet is aimed at data science and ML engineering teams that need experiment tracking and model governance, as well as AI teams building and debugging LLM applications and agents with Opik. Both products offer free tiers, with paid Pro and custom Enterprise plans for larger teams needing higher usage limits, compliance certifications, and dedicated support.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source (Apache 2.0) framework for evaluating, testing, and monitoring LLMs, RAG apps, AI agents, and ML models.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Evidently AI is an open-source (Apache 2.0) library and platform for evaluating, testing, and monitoring LLMs, RAG systems, AI agents, and ML models.

features.title: Automated Evaluation
features.description: Measure output quality, safety, and reliability with shareable visual reports.

features.title: 100+ Built-in Metrics
features.description: Pre-built checks covering hallucinations, PII detection, toxicity, retrieval quality, and custom evaluations.

features.title: Synthetic Data Generation
features.description: Create realistic, edge-case, or adversarial test inputs for stress-testing AI systems.

features.title: Continuous Monitoring
features.description: Live dashboards that track quality checks and detect drift or regressions over time.

features.title: Tracing / Instrumentation
features.description: Instrument LLM and RAG applications to capture data for evaluation.

features.title: Open-Source Python Library
features.description: Free, self-hostable core evaluation library released under the Apache 2.0 license.

pros: Fully open source under the permissive Apache 2.0 license
Large, active community with 7,500+ GitHub stars and 40M+ downloads
Covers both traditional ML monitoring and modern LLM/RAG/agent evaluation in a single framework
100+ pre-built metrics reduce the need to write custom evaluation logic
Used by companies including DeepL, Wise, Realtor.com, and Plaid, per Evidently's website
cons: Premium features such as no-code dashboards, alerting/scheduling, and role-based access control require the paid Cloud/Enterprise platform, not the free OSS library
Public pricing for the Cloud/Enterprise platform is not listed on the website; requires contacting Evidently for a quote
Self-managing the open-source deployment requires handling your own infrastructure and support

faqs.question: Is Evidently free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Evidently library is fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to use and self-host.

faqs.question: Does Evidently offer a hosted or cloud option?
faqs.answer: Yes, Evidently offers a commercial Platform, available as cloud-hosted or self-hosted Enterprise, with additional no-code features, dashboards, and support on top of the open-source library.

faqs.question: What can Evidently evaluate?
faqs.answer: Evidently can evaluate LLMs, RAG applications, AI agents, and traditional ML models, checking for issues like hallucinations, PII exposure, toxicity, and data drift.

faqs.question: How is Evidently licensed?
faqs.answer: The open-source library is licensed under Apache 2.0.

faqs.question: Who uses Evidently?
faqs.answer: Evidently states it is used by thousands of companies from startups to enterprises, including DeepL, Wise, Realtor.com, and Plaid.

use_cases.title: LLM/RAG evaluation
use_cases.description: AI teams evaluate LLM and RAG application outputs for quality, hallucinations, and safety before and after deployment.

use_cases.title: ML model monitoring
use_cases.description: Data science teams track data drift and model performance degradation in production.

use_cases.title: Synthetic test data creation
use_cases.description: Teams generate edge-case and adversarial test inputs to stress-test AI systems before release.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted, open-source evaluation
use_cases.description: Developers who need a free, self-hosted evaluation library integrate Evidently directly into their pipelines.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Evidently?
paragraphs: Evidently is an open-source framework, founded in 2020, for evaluating, testing, and monitoring AI systems, including LLMs, RAG applications, AI agents, and traditional ML models. The core library is released under the Apache 2.0 license and includes 100+ built-in metrics covering issues such as hallucinations, PII detection, toxicity, retrieval quality, and data drift.

Beyond the open-source library, Evidently also offers a commercial Platform, available as a managed cloud service or self-hosted Enterprise deployment, that adds no-code dashboards, synthetic data generation, alerting, scheduled automation, and role-based access control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Evidently is for
paragraphs: Evidently is used by individual developers and data science teams who want a free, self-hostable evaluation library, as well as larger organizations that need the managed Platform's no-code workflows, dashboards, and access controls. The company states its tools are used by thousands of companies, from startups to enterprises such as DeepL, Wise, Realtor.com, and Plaid.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source AI observability platform by Arize AI for tracing, evaluating, and debugging LLM applications and agents.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Arize Phoenix is an open-source LLM observability platform for tracing, evaluation, experimentation, and prompt iteration, maintained by Arize AI.

features.title: OpenTelemetry-Based Tracing
features.description: Runtime monitoring and tracing of LLM application calls using OpenTelemetry instrumentation.

features.title: LLM-Based Evaluation
features.description: Benchmark application performance using LLM-based assessments.

features.title: Versioned Datasets
features.description: Manage versioned example collections for experimentation and fine-tuning.

features.title: Experiments
features.description: Track and evaluate changes to prompts and models over time.

features.title: Prompt Playground
features.description: Interactively test, optimize, and replay traced LLM calls.

features.title: Prompt Management
features.description: Version control and systematic iteration on prompts.

features.title: Broad Framework Support
features.description: Out-of-the-box instrumentation for LangChain, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, DSPy, and major LLM providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and AWS Bedrock.

pros: Free and open source with no feature gates for self-hosting
Local-first: runs on a laptop, in a Jupyter notebook, in a container, or in the cloud
Backed and maintained by Arize AI, an established AI observability company
Broad integration support across LLM frameworks and providers via OpenTelemetry-based instrumentation
Active project with 10.6k+ GitHub stars
cons: Licensed under Elastic License 2.0 (ELv2), which is not an OSI-approved open-source license and prohibits offering Phoenix as a hosted or managed service to third parties
No dedicated Phoenix cloud/SaaS tier; teams needing a fully managed commercial product must move to Arize's separate paid product, Arize AX
Portions of the codebase are patent-protected under U.S. patents per the project's IP notice

faqs.question: Is Arize Phoenix free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Phoenix is free to self-host with no feature gates, though it is licensed under Elastic License 2.0 rather than a fully permissive open-source license.

faqs.question: Who maintains Arize Phoenix?
faqs.answer: Phoenix is maintained by Arize AI, founded in 2020 and headquartered in Berkeley, California.

faqs.question: Can Phoenix be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Phoenix can run locally via pip or conda, in a Jupyter notebook, in a container, or deployed to platforms like Railway, Render, Google Cloud, Azure, and AWS.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Phoenix and Arize AX?
faqs.answer: Phoenix is Arize's open-source, local-first observability and evaluation tool; Arize AX is Arize's separate commercial, fully managed observability platform.

faqs.question: What license does Phoenix use?
faqs.answer: Phoenix is released under the Elastic License 2.0 (ELv2), which is free to self-host but restricts offering it as a hosted or managed service.

use_cases.title: LLM application tracing
use_cases.description: Developers instrument LLM applications to trace and debug requests end-to-end.

use_cases.title: RAG pipeline debugging
use_cases.description: Teams evaluate retrieval quality and debug RAG pipelines using versioned datasets and experiments.

use_cases.title: Prompt iteration
use_cases.description: Teams use the playground to test and refine prompts before deployment.

use_cases.title: Local or offline LLM evaluation
use_cases.description: Individual developers run Phoenix locally or in notebooks for quick, self-contained evaluation without a cloud account.

features: Full tracing, evaluation, experiments, and playground
Deployable locally, in notebooks, containers, or the cloud

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Arize Phoenix?
paragraphs: Arize Phoenix is an open-source, local-first AI observability platform maintained by Arize AI, a company founded in 2020 and headquartered in Berkeley, California. Phoenix provides OpenTelemetry-based tracing, LLM-based evaluation, versioned datasets, experiments, a prompt playground, and prompt management for LLM applications and agents.

Phoenix is released under the Elastic License 2.0 (ELv2), a source-available license that lets anyone self-host it for free with no feature gates, but that restricts offering Phoenix itself as a hosted or managed service. Teams that need a fully managed, commercial version of Arize's observability tooling instead use Arize's separate paid product, Arize AX.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Arize Phoenix is for
paragraphs: Phoenix is aimed at developers and AI teams who want a free, self-hosted or local-first way to trace, evaluate, and debug LLM applications, RAG pipelines, and agents, without adopting a cloud subscription. It supports popular frameworks such as LangChain, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, and DSPy, as well as providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and AWS Bedrock.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source (MIT core) LLM engineering platform for tracing, evaluation, and prompt management, with free self-hosting and paid cloud tiers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Langfuse is an open-source (YC W23) LLM engineering platform for tracing, evaluation, and prompt management, with free self-hosting and paid cloud plans.

features.title: LLM Tracing & Observability
features.description: Track LLM calls, multi-turn sessions, and visualize agents as graphs.

features.title: Prompt Management
features.description: Version, collaboratively edit, and deploy prompts without code changes.

features.title: Evaluation
features.description: LLM-as-a-judge, code-based evaluators, user feedback, and manual labeling for development and production.

features.title: Datasets
features.description: Systematic testing to validate application performance across scenarios.

features.title: LLM Playground
features.description: Interactively test and iterate on prompts.

features.title: Open Source & Self-Hostable
features.description: MIT-licensed core, deployable via Docker or Kubernetes.

features.title: Broad Integrations
features.description: Native Python/JS SDKs, 100+ library integrations, OpenTelemetry, and LLM gateway support such as LiteLLM.

pros: MIT-licensed core with no usage limits on tracing, evaluation, prompt management, and more
Free, unlimited self-hosting via Docker or Kubernetes
Y Combinator-backed (W23) with active, ongoing development
Cloud plans include a free Hobby tier with no credit card required
Broad integration ecosystem, including OpenTelemetry, LangChain, LlamaIndex, and the OpenAI SDK
cons: Certain enterprise features (SCIM, audit logging, data retention policies) require a commercial license even when self-hosting
Cloud pricing scales quickly: the Enterprise plan starts at $2,499/month
Usage-based overage charges apply beyond included units on paid cloud plans
SSO and fine-grained access control require an additional Teams add-on ($300/month) on the Pro plan

faqs.question: Is Langfuse free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The core of Langfuse is MIT licensed and free to self-host without usage limits, and there is also a free Hobby cloud plan with no credit card required.

faqs.question: Can Langfuse be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Langfuse is open source and self-hostable for free via Docker or Kubernetes, though some enterprise features require a commercial license.

faqs.question: Who founded Langfuse?
faqs.answer: Langfuse was founded in 2022 by Marc Klingen, Clemens Rawert, and Maximilian Deichmann, and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch.

faqs.question: Where is Langfuse based?
faqs.answer: Langfuse has offices in Berlin, Germany for product and engineering, and San Francisco, California for go-to-market.

faqs.question: What are Langfuse's cloud pricing plans?
faqs.answer: Langfuse Cloud offers a free Hobby plan, Core at $29/month, Pro at $199/month, and Enterprise starting at $2,499/month, plus usage-based overage pricing.

use_cases.title: LLM application debugging
use_cases.description: Teams trace and analyze multi-turn LLM conversations to debug production issues.

use_cases.title: Prompt lifecycle management
use_cases.description: Teams version, test, and deploy prompts collaboratively without redeploying code.

use_cases.title: LLM evaluation and quality assurance
use_cases.description: Teams run LLM-as-a-judge and human evaluations to score application outputs.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted LLM observability
use_cases.description: Organizations with data residency or compliance needs self-host Langfuse for free using Docker or Kubernetes.

features: Optional Teams add-on ($300/month) for SSO and fine-grained access control
Custom pricing available with yearly commitment

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Langfuse?
paragraphs: Langfuse is an open-source LLM engineering platform founded in 2022 by Marc Klingen, Clemens Rawert, and Maximilian Deichmann, and part of Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch. It helps teams debug, analyze, and iterate on LLM applications through tracing, evaluation, prompt management, and datasets. Its core is MIT licensed, with the full product feature set, including tracing, evaluations, prompt management, and the playground, available without usage limits on self-hosted deployments.

Langfuse also offers a managed Langfuse Cloud with a free Hobby tier plus paid Core, Pro, and Enterprise plans that add higher usage limits, longer data retention, and enterprise features such as SSO and audit logs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Langfuse is for
paragraphs: Langfuse is built for AI engineering and product teams building LLM applications who need observability, evaluation, and prompt management, from individual developers self-hosting the free MIT-licensed core to enterprises using Langfuse Cloud's paid plans for compliance features like audit logging and long-term data retention.

enrichment.short_description: Helicone is an open-source AI gateway and LLM observability platform for logging, debugging, and analyzing LLM application traffic.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Helicone is an open-source AI gateway and LLM observability platform with request logging, prompt management, and a free self-hosted option.

features.title: AI Gateway
features.description: Unified OpenAI-compatible API for accessing 100+ LLM models across OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and other providers.

features.title: Request logging and tracing
features.description: Automatically logs and traces requests, agents, and chatbot sessions with dashboard analytics visible within seconds.

features.title: Prompt management
features.description: Version, deploy, and manage prompts with a built-in playground for testing.

features.title: Segments, sessions and user tracking
features.description: Groups requests into sessions and tracks usage by user or segment.

features.title: HQL (Helicone Query Language)
features.description: A dedicated query language for analyzing logged request data.

features.title: Rate limiting and alerts
features.description: Set rate limits on API usage and configure alerts for anomalies or thresholds.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Fully open source under Apache 2.0, deployable via Docker Compose for local setups or a Helm chart for enterprise deployments.

features.title: Cost and latency analytics
features.description: Tracks cost and latency across providers with PostHog integration for further analysis.

pros: Open source (Apache 2.0) with self-hosting available via Docker Compose or an enterprise Helm chart
Free Hobby tier includes 10,000 requests per month with no cost
0% markup on LLM provider costs when used as an AI gateway
Backed by Y Combinator (W23) with an active GitHub repository (6,000+ stars)
SOC 2 and GDPR compliance, with HIPAA available on higher tiers
cons: Paid Pro and Team plans include usage-based charges beyond the free allowances
Enterprise features like on-premises deployment and SAML SSO require custom quote-only pricing
Data retention on lower tiers is limited (7 days on the free Hobby plan)

faqs.question: Is Helicone free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Helicone has a free Hobby plan with 10,000 requests per month, 1 GB storage, and 7-day data retention. It is also fully open source and can be self-hosted at no cost.

faqs.question: Can Helicone be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. Helicone is open source under the Apache 2.0 license and can be self-hosted via Docker Compose, with a production-ready Helm chart available for enterprise deployments.

faqs.question: What does the Pro plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan starts at $79/month and includes unlimited seats, alerts, reports, and the HQL query language, plus a 7-day free trial.

faqs.question: Does Helicone charge extra to route LLM requests?
faqs.answer: Helicone advertises 0% markup on LLM provider costs when used as an AI gateway; billing is based on the platform's own request/storage tiers instead.

faqs.question: Which LLM providers does Helicone support?
faqs.answer: Helicone integrates with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google (Vertex/Gemini), Azure, Groq, Together AI, OpenRouter, LiteLLM, Anyscale, Mistral, DeepSeek, and other providers.

use_cases.title: LLM request logging and debugging
use_cases.description: Teams that need visibility into every LLM API call, including latency, cost, and full request/response payloads.

use_cases.title: Unified multi-provider access
use_cases.description: Companies wanting a single OpenAI-compatible API to route across 100+ models from different providers with automatic failover.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted LLM observability
use_cases.description: Organizations with data residency or compliance requirements that need to run observability infrastructure on their own servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Helicone?
paragraphs: Helicone is an open-source AI gateway and LLM observability platform that lets developers route, log, debug, and analyze traffic from their LLM applications. It provides an OpenAI-compatible unified API for accessing 100+ models across providers such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google, alongside a dashboard for request tracing, cost and latency analytics, prompt management, and alerting.

The project is released under the Apache 2.0 license and can be self-hosted via Docker Compose or, for enterprise deployments, a Helm chart. Helicone is backed by Y Combinator (W23 batch) and is headquartered in San Francisco.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Helicone is for
paragraphs: Helicone suits AI engineering teams that need visibility into LLM API usage, cost, and latency, as well as teams that want a single gateway to route requests across multiple LLM providers. Its free self-hosted option also appeals to organizations with data residency requirements, while its paid Pro and Team plans target teams that need collaboration features, longer data retention, and compliance certifications.

enrichment.short_description: OpenLLMetry is an open-source, OpenTelemetry-based SDK by Traceloop for instrumenting and observing LLM and GenAI applications.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenLLMetry is a free, open-source OpenTelemetry SDK for instrumenting LLM applications, maintained by Traceloop under Apache 2.0.

features.title: OpenTelemetry-based instrumentation
features.description: Extends OpenTelemetry with LLM-specific instrumentation so teams can reuse existing observability infrastructure.

features.title: Two-line setup
features.description: Instrument an LLM application with as little as two lines of code.

features.title: Python and TypeScript SDKs
features.description: Official SDKs available for both Python and Node.js/TypeScript applications.

features.title: Broad LLM provider support
features.description: Instrumentations for OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS Bedrock, Cohere, Google Gemini, Mistral AI, Ollama, Together.ai, Watsonx, and more.

features.title: Vector database and framework support
features.description: Supports vector databases such as Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, and Chroma, plus frameworks like LangChain, LlamaIndex, and CrewAI.

features.title: Exports to standard observability platforms
features.description: Traces can be sent to Datadog, Honeycomb, New Relic, Grafana Tempo, Splunk, Dynatrace, and other OpenTelemetry-compatible backends.

features.title: Workflow decorators
features.description: Provides decorators for tracking and labeling LLM workflow steps in code.

pros: Fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license, free to use with no vendor lock-in
Built on the OpenTelemetry standard, so traces integrate with existing observability tooling
Broad ecosystem of provider, vector-database, and observability-platform integrations
Actively maintained on GitHub (7,000+ stars) with an active Slack and GitHub Discussions community
cons: As of March 2026, Traceloop (the company maintaining OpenLLMetry) was acquired by ServiceNow, which introduces uncertainty about the open-source project's long-term independent roadmap
The SDK itself has no built-in hosted dashboard; teams need a separate OpenTelemetry-compatible backend or the Traceloop platform to visualize traces
Requires OpenTelemetry familiarity to get the most out of custom configurations

faqs.question: Is OpenLLMetry free?
faqs.answer: Yes. OpenLLMetry is open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to use.

faqs.question: What languages does OpenLLMetry support?
faqs.answer: OpenLLMetry provides official SDKs for Python and TypeScript/Node.js.

faqs.question: Who maintains OpenLLMetry?
faqs.answer: OpenLLMetry is built and maintained by Traceloop, which was acquired by ServiceNow in March 2026; the open-source project remains available on GitHub under Apache 2.0.

faqs.question: Does OpenLLMetry require a specific observability backend?
faqs.answer: No. Because it is built on OpenTelemetry, traces can be exported to any OpenTelemetry-compatible backend, including Datadog, Honeycomb, New Relic, and Traceloop's own platform.

faqs.question: How do I install OpenLLMetry?
faqs.answer: For Python, install the traceloop-sdk package and initialize it with Traceloop.init() in your application code.

use_cases.title: Instrumenting LLM applications with OpenTelemetry
use_cases.description: Teams that already use OpenTelemetry and want to extend it to trace LLM calls, prompts, and completions.

use_cases.title: Multi-provider LLM tracing
use_cases.description: Applications calling multiple LLM providers or vector databases that need consistent tracing across all of them.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is OpenLLMetry?
paragraphs: OpenLLMetry is an open-source SDK, released under the Apache 2.0 license, that extends OpenTelemetry with instrumentation for LLM and GenAI applications. It lets developers add tracing to LLM calls, prompts, vector database queries, and framework operations (such as LangChain or LlamaIndex) with minimal code changes, then export those traces to any OpenTelemetry-compatible observability platform.

The project is built and maintained by Traceloop, which ships OpenLLMetry as the open-source foundation of its own LLM observability platform. In March 2026, Traceloop was acquired by ServiceNow and its technology became part of ServiceNow's AI Control Tower; the OpenLLMetry GitHub repository remains publicly available under its open-source license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who OpenLLMetry is for
paragraphs: OpenLLMetry is suited to engineering teams that already rely on OpenTelemetry-based observability and want to extend the same tracing pipeline to their LLM and agent workloads, rather than adopting a separate proprietary logging tool. It is free to use for teams comfortable self-managing instrumentation and choosing their own trace backend.

enrichment.short_description: PromptLayer is a prompt management, evaluation, and LLM observability platform for AI engineering teams, founded in 2021.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PromptLayer offers prompt management, evaluations, and LLM observability, with a free plan and paid tiers starting at $49/month.

features.title: Prompt registry (CMS)
features.description: Centralized, dynamically-fetched prompt registry — update prompts in the dashboard and applications pick up new versions without redeploying.

features.title: Automatic versioning
features.description: Every LLM call creates a version in the registry, preserving history without requiring manual saves.

features.title: Evaluation harness
features.description: Supports backtesting against production examples, regression testing, and CI-connected evaluation pipelines that run automatically on new prompt versions.

features.title: Multiple scoring methods
features.description: Evaluations can use LLM-as-judge, human grading, equality comparison, cosine similarity, or custom code evaluators.

features.title: Observability and request logging
features.description: Captures every LLM API call with full request/response payloads, latency, and cost, tied back to the prompt version that served it.

features.title: Request replay
features.description: Replays logged requests to debug issues or test new prompt variations against historical data.

features.title: Release and dynamic labels
features.description: Promote prompt versions between environments using labels, with commit messages and diffs on each version.

features.title: Non-developer collaboration
features.description: Lets domain experts edit and manage prompts without modifying application code.

pros: Free plan available with no cost for smaller usage (2,500 requests/month, 5 users)
Combines prompt versioning, evaluations, and observability in a single registry-first platform
Real-time monitoring of token usage, model performance, error rates, and cost
Enterprise plan offers flexible/self-hosted deployment options and HIPAA with BAA
cons: Paid plans add pay-as-you-go transaction fees on top of the base subscription ($0.003 or $0.002 per transaction)
Free plan is limited to 10 prompts and 10 playground runs per day
Enterprise-grade features like RBAC, deployment approvals, and self-hosting require custom/quote-only Enterprise pricing

faqs.question: Is PromptLayer free?
faqs.answer: Yes, PromptLayer offers a free plan for up to 5 users with 2,500 requests/month, 1 workspace, and 10 prompts.

faqs.question: How much does PromptLayer cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan starts at $49/month, and the Team plan is $500/month; both add pay-as-you-go transaction fees. Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: When was PromptLayer founded?
faqs.answer: PromptLayer was founded in 2021.

faqs.question: Does PromptLayer support self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Flexible hosting options, including self-hosted deployment, are available on the Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: What can PromptLayer evaluations use for scoring?
faqs.answer: Evaluations can be scored using LLM-as-judge, human grading, equality comparison, cosine similarity, or custom code evaluators.

use_cases.title: Prompt version control and collaboration
use_cases.description: Teams that need a shared registry so both engineers and non-technical domain experts can edit and deploy prompts without code changes.

use_cases.title: Regression testing for prompt changes
use_cases.description: Running backtests and CI-connected evaluation pipelines to catch regressions before a new prompt version ships.

use_cases.title: Production LLM monitoring
use_cases.description: Tracking cost, latency, token usage, and error rates for LLM calls tied back to specific prompt versions.
use_cases.audience: Teams operating LLM applications in production

long_form_blocks.heading: What is PromptLayer?
paragraphs: PromptLayer is a prompt management, evaluation, and observability platform for AI engineering teams. It centers on a prompt registry (CMS) where every LLM call automatically creates a new version, so teams can update prompts in a dashboard and have applications pick up changes without redeploying code.

Beyond versioning, PromptLayer includes an evaluation harness for backtesting and regression testing prompt changes against production data, plus request-level observability that logs cost, latency, and full payloads tied back to specific prompt versions. PromptLayer was founded in 2021 and is based in New York City.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who PromptLayer is for
paragraphs: PromptLayer is built for AI engineering teams that want a shared, non-developer-friendly system for managing, testing, and monitoring prompts in production, rather than building this tooling in-house. Its free plan suits individuals and small projects, while Pro and Team plans target growing teams, and the Enterprise plan serves organizations needing SSO, RBAC, and flexible hosting.

name: Humanloop
skip_reason: Humanloop was acquired by Anthropic in August 2025 and the standalone platform was sunset on September 8, 2025; its UI and API are no longer available and the team was absorbed into Anthropic Console, so there is no live product to enrich accurately.

enrichment.short_description: LangSmith is LangChain's platform for tracing, monitoring, evaluating, and debugging LLM applications and AI agents in production.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LangSmith is LangChain's observability and evaluation platform for tracing, monitoring, and debugging LLM applications and AI agents.

features.title: Tracing
features.description: Step-by-step visibility into what an agent is doing, with native support for popular LLM frameworks.

features.title: Production monitoring
features.description: Real-time dashboards tracking cost, latency (P50/P99), and error rates with configurable alerts.

features.title: Evaluations
features.description: LLM-as-judge and code-based evaluations to test and score application quality.

features.title: Automatic insights
features.description: Trace clustering that surfaces patterns and recurring failure modes across large volumes of traces.

features.title: Framework-agnostic SDKs
features.description: SDKs for Python, TypeScript, Go, and Java, with integrations for LangChain, LlamaIndex, OpenAI SDK, Anthropic SDK, Vercel AI SDK, and OpenTelemetry.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Available as managed cloud, bring-your-own-cloud (BYOC) on AWS/GCP/Azure, or self-hosted on Kubernetes.

pros: Framework-agnostic — works with LangChain, LlamaIndex, raw OpenAI/Anthropic SDKs, and OpenTelemetry
Free Developer tier available with no credit card required to start
SDKs across Python, TypeScript, Go, and Java for broad language coverage
Enterprise deployment options including self-hosted and BYOC for data residency needs
Discounted rates and free trace allotments offered to VC-backed startups
cons: Usage-based pricing (compute and storage units) can make costs unpredictable at scale beyond base trace allotments
Free Developer plan is capped at 1 seat and 5,000 base traces per month
Self-hosted and BYOC options require Enterprise-tier custom pricing
Extended trace retention beyond 14 days requires an upgrade

faqs.question: Is LangSmith free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, LangSmith has a free Developer plan with up to 5,000 base traces per month and 1 seat, billed pay-as-you-go for additional usage.

faqs.question: Does LangSmith require LangChain?
faqs.answer: No, LangSmith is framework-agnostic and works with LangChain, LlamaIndex, the OpenAI SDK, the Anthropic SDK, the Vercel AI SDK, and OpenTelemetry.

faqs.question: Can LangSmith be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, LangSmith Enterprise supports self-hosted deployment on Kubernetes as well as bring-your-own-cloud (BYOC) on AWS, GCP, or Azure.

faqs.question: What does LangSmith cost beyond the free plan?
faqs.answer: The Plus plan starts at $39 per seat per month plus usage charges for compute (LCUs) and storage (LSUs); Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Who built LangSmith?
faqs.answer: LangSmith is built by LangChain, Inc., the company behind the LangChain open-source framework, headquartered in San Francisco.

use_cases.title: Debugging LLM agents
use_cases.description: Trace every step an agent takes to identify where a response went wrong.

use_cases.title: Production monitoring
use_cases.description: Track cost, latency, and error rates for LLM applications running in production with configurable alerts.
use_cases.audience: Engineering and platform teams operating LLM apps at scale

use_cases.title: Systematic evaluation
use_cases.description: Run LLM-as-judge or code-based evaluations to score application quality before and after changes.
use_cases.audience: ML and QA teams validating model or prompt changes

use_cases.title: Enterprise-grade deployment
use_cases.description: Self-host or bring your own cloud to keep trace data within a private environment.
use_cases.audience: Enterprises with data residency or compliance requirements

long_form_blocks.heading: What is LangSmith?
paragraphs: LangSmith is an observability and evaluation platform from LangChain, Inc. that gives teams visibility into how LLM applications and AI agents behave in production. It provides step-by-step tracing, real-time monitoring dashboards for cost, latency, and error rates, and automatic clustering of traces to surface recurring failure patterns.

The platform is framework-agnostic, with SDKs for Python, TypeScript, Go, and Java, and integrations for LangChain, LlamaIndex, the OpenAI and Anthropic SDKs, the Vercel AI SDK, and OpenTelemetry. It can be run as a managed cloud service, on a customer's own cloud (BYOC across AWS, GCP, or Azure), or fully self-hosted on Kubernetes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who LangSmith is for
paragraphs: LangSmith is built for engineering and ML teams building and operating LLM-powered applications and agents, from individual developers debugging locally on the free Developer plan to enterprises that need self-hosted or BYOC deployment for data residency and compliance.

enrichment.short_description: Dify is an open-source platform for visually building, testing, and deploying AI agents, workflows, and RAG pipelines.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dify is an open-source platform for building AI agents, agentic workflows, and RAG pipelines with a visual workflow studio and self-hosting option.

features.title: Workflow Studio
features.description: Visual, node-based builder for creating agentic workflows without managing infrastructure.

features.title: Agent building
features.description: Configure agents with memory, tools, and defined boundaries.

features.title: Knowledge pipeline
features.description: Document processing and indexing to build retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) knowledge bases.

features.title: Marketplace
features.description: Access to model providers, tools, data sources, and MCP-compatible integrations.

features.title: Multiple publishing options
features.description: Deploy apps as web apps, REST APIs, embeds, or MCP-compatible tools.

features.title: Monitoring
features.description: Logs, user feedback, latency, and usage tracking for deployed apps.

features.title: Plugin development
features.description: Framework for building custom tools, models, and integrations, plus a difyctl CLI for CI/CD workflows.

pros: Open-source Community Edition can be self-hosted via Docker Compose at no cost
149K+ GitHub stars and an active open-source community
Free Dify Cloud Sandbox tier available with no installation required
Deployment flexibility: managed cloud, self-hosted, or private/VPC Enterprise deployment
Enterprise tier offers SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 compliance plus SSO/SAML and RBAC
cons: Source code is released under a modified Apache 2.0 license that requires a commercial license for multi-tenant SaaS use
Self-hosting the Community Edition requires Docker and technical setup
Paid Cloud plans (Professional, Team) are billed annually, with no listed monthly option
Enterprise pricing is custom and not publicly listed

faqs.question: Is Dify free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Community Edition is open source and free to self-host, and Dify Cloud offers a free Sandbox tier with 200 message credits and limited usage.

faqs.question: Can Dify be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source Community Edition can be self-hosted with Docker Compose, and an Enterprise self-hosted option with VPC deployment is also available.

faqs.question: What license does Dify use?
faqs.answer: Dify is released under a modified version of the Apache License 2.0 that permits commercial use but requires a commercial license for offering Dify as a multi-tenant SaaS service.

faqs.question: How much does Dify Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Dify Cloud's Professional plan starts at $590/year and the Team plan is $1,590/year; a free Sandbox tier and custom Enterprise pricing are also available.

faqs.question: Who makes Dify?
faqs.answer: Dify is developed by LangGenius, headquartered in Menlo Park, California, with additional teams in Tokyo, Suzhou, and Shanghai.

use_cases.title: Building AI agents visually
use_cases.description: Assemble agents with memory, tools, and defined behavior boundaries using a no-code workflow builder.
use_cases.audience: Product teams and developers building AI agents without heavy infrastructure work

use_cases.title: RAG knowledge bases
use_cases.description: Process and index documents into a knowledge pipeline that agents can retrieve from.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted AI deployment
use_cases.description: Run the open-source Community Edition on private infrastructure via Docker Compose.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams that need full control over data and hosting

use_cases.title: Enterprise AI app deployment
use_cases.description: Deploy with VPC isolation, SSO/SAML, RBAC, and audit logs under a commercial license.
use_cases.audience: Enterprises with compliance and security requirements

features: All core features under the public repository
SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 compliance

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Dify?
paragraphs: Dify is an open-source platform for building production-ready AI agents, agentic workflows, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines. Its visual Workflow Studio lets teams connect nodes to build agent logic without managing underlying infrastructure, and a knowledge pipeline handles document processing for indexed knowledge bases.

The project's Community Edition is released under a modified Apache License 2.0 and can be self-hosted with Docker Compose, while Dify Cloud offers a managed SaaS with a free Sandbox tier plus paid Professional and Team plans. A separate Enterprise offering adds VPC/private deployment, SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, and SOC 2 Type II plus ISO 27001 compliance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Dify is for
paragraphs: Dify suits developers and product teams who want to build AI agents and RAG applications visually, open-source users who want to self-host on their own infrastructure, and enterprises that need compliance-grade private deployment with SSO and audit logging.
